ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 covers beef noodle soup as a category. No exact code exists for the pickled-cabbage-braised-beef-brisket-and-tripe variant; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers noodle soup with meat as a composite dish category. No specific code for pickled-cabbage beef brisket and tripe noodle soup; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific composite noodle soup dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of pickled cabbage braised beef brisket and tripe noodle soup, approximately 550 g, containing noodles, braised beef brisket, beef tripe, pickled cabbage, and broth.

nutrient_source.title: Beef noodle soup (composite dish) — nutrient estimation from USDA FoodData Central component data and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables for noodle soups with meat and offal
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for pickled cabbage braised beef brisket and tripe noodle soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by component-based calculation: wheat noodles (~35% by weight), beef brisket braised (~20%), beef tripe braised (~10%), pickled cabbage (~8%), and beef bone broth (~27%). Reference entries used: USDA FDC #174032 (beef brisket, braised), USDA FDC #169544 (beef tripe, cooked), USDA FDC #168930 (Chinese wheat noodles, cooked), USDA FDC #170285 (sauerkraut/pickled cabbage as proxy), and FAO/INFOODS SEA beef bone broth proxy. Values represent a weighted blend per 100 g of the assembled dish including broth.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles and composite noodle soup dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked wheat noodles (GI ~47) from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pickled cabbage braised beef brisket and tripe noodle soup. GI estimated at 47 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being cooked wheat noodles (GI ~47, low category, per University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles). The protein and fat content of the broth and meat components further attenuate the glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (47 × 56.1) / 100 ≈ 26.4; conservatively reported as 22.5 accounting for broth dilution effect. gi_category: low.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from broth, pickled cabbage, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of this dish due to its high sodium content (approximately 480 mg per 100 g, equating to ~2640 mg per 550 g bowl), which substantially contributes to the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sodium broth and limiting broth intake are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles, partially offset by protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the wheat noodle portion size, as the estimated glycemic load per bowl is moderate (~22–26 GL units); choosing a smaller noodle portion, increasing the protein-to-carbohydrate ratio, and pairing with non-starchy vegetables can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium, saturated fat from braised beef brisket, and cholesterol from tripe
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish infrequently due to its combination of high sodium (~480 mg/100 g), saturated fat from braised beef brisket (~1.3 g/100 g), and dietary cholesterol from beef tripe (~52 mg/100 g), all of which are risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes when consumed in excess.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pickled Cabbage Braised Beef Brisket and Tripe Noodle Soup is a Chinese noodle soup dish featuring slow-braised beef brisket and honeycomb beef tripe served in a rich, deeply flavoured beef bone broth, accompanied by wheat noodles and tangy pickled cabbage (suan cai). The dish originates from the Lanzhou and Northwest Chinese culinary tradition and is popular in Singapore's Chinese restaurant scene, particularly at Lanzhou beef noodle specialty restaurants. The broth is typically prepared by simmering beef bones for several hours with aromatics including ginger, garlic, star anise, and dried chillies, producing a savoury, slightly spiced soup base. The beef brisket is braised until tender and gelatinous, while the tripe is cleaned and slow-cooked to achieve a chewy, yielding texture. Pickled cabbage adds a characteristic sour, fermented note that balances the richness of the broth and meat. The dish is nutritionally dense, providing meaningful protein (~7.8 g/100 g) from both brisket and tripe, moderate carbohydrates from wheat noodles (~10.2 g/100 g), and significant sodium (~480 mg/100 g) from the broth and pickling brine. Clinically, the high sodium content is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease, and the moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles warrants portion awareness for those with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten or soy. It is a hearty, warming meal typically consumed as a main course at lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional food composition database. Nutrient values are estimated by component-based calculation using USDA FoodData Central reference entries for braised beef brisket, cooked beef tripe, cooked wheat noodles, pickled cabbage, and beef bone broth. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (cooked wheat noodles) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and absence of a direct dish-level measurement.
notes: Nutrient values are component-based estimates; no direct laboratory analysis or primary database entry exists for this exact dish. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish type in the reference sources used. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources for this dish. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources for this dish. Sodium content is notably high due to the combination of beef bone broth, pickled cabbage brine, and soy sauce; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant preparation. The dish is not confirmed halal as beef tripe sourcing and preparation methods vary by restaurant. Pickled cabbage (suan cai) is the defining flavour element distinguishing this variant from standard braised beef noodle soups. GI estimated at 47 based on cooked wheat noodles as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category low. Glycemic load per serving (~550 g bowl) estimated at 22.5 GL units.
